this is my resume roast it as much as you want...!!!

Your resume is seriously impressive, especially that 9.24 CGPA, great job! I've got a few ideas that could take it to the next level. First, for your projects, try to quantify the results even more. For example, instead of just saying you "implemented a robust authentication system," you could highlight how it secured the API and enabled features like session management. Also, when you describe your GoBMS, you could really emphasize the custom SQL-like query engine and secondary indexing you built, which shows some serious technical depth. Finally, the 95% accuracy for your TimeKeeper app is a fantastic detail; you should look for ways to add similar metrics to your other projects to really show their impact. Just a few tweaks, and this resume will be flawless!

Hey guys what do you think about this resume, 
Is this at least 6-7/10 for a 3rd year student??
Heyy , Your projects are good and programming skills are impressive too , but you can show them better

see for one , remove any words like "passionate" , "motivated" etc . these are called buzzwords , ask chatgpt to write the summary for you but give him a honest backstory about yourself first and then ask for no buzzwords , simple vocab and to keep it chill

then the projects , it is good that they are the first thing but , i think you can still format them better , like quantifying your achievements , like  improved compile time by x% , or tested by x+ early adapters , giving a average rating of x/5
also use bullet points instead of paragraphs , helps people parse or skim through them better
you can ask chatgpt to do that for you as well , and as someone told me here , use latex code for building a resume and ask chatgpt to write the latex code as well
yo , so i did this a while ago , but i think the resume now is looking pretty good , might as well give some different prespectives

messages.content:
Need some advice on my current resume
Will be applying for full stack/software development/ML intern roles from next month
I don’t have much to add since I am a student myself, but I would like to point out a small error. In your skills, you listed 'Next.js' twice under the web frameworks section.
Thanks @bruh__h but I think you mistook Nest.js as Next.js
my bad bro
It's alright bro. At least you read my resume

1. Get rid of the icons
2. Instead of attaching links to a text/icon directly enter the link without `https`. Many softwares out there just extract the text from the PDF and attached link isn't a part of the text 😩 so the bot will get some gibberish for icons and no external links for your profile and projects
3. Order > Skills, Education, Experience, Projects
4. Add twitter etc only if there's something to show, like if you tweet POSITIVELY about tech
5. Attached image - The bullet point is not alligned with the text. Also match that bullet point in project section with the experience and education section. Inshort keep it consistent across the page
6. You can actually explain the project you worked on and then the work that you did on that project in your experience, check the attached screenshot for reference.
7. I won't suggest keeping specialised area in skills and you've already mentioned these things in your objective
8. If you can, add live URLs for your projects if they're website or add demo video on the github readme. Your resume goes through non tech people.

Overall good resume 😌
Thanks for taking the time to review my resume. I'll update accordingly.

hey guys can you give me a quick resume roast?
i ain't getting any interviews and i just changed to this from an older version today what can i improve

Put skills above experience
Order skills in descending order of proficiency (Skill 1 > skill 2 > skill 3 if you are better at 1 than at 2 than at 3)
Remove the actual time of your API latency (10 seconds still doesn't sound very efficient, which may or may not be true given your use case but sends the wrong message)
Link company name to company website

all the best!

ill just remove the seconds the i guess
yes, definitely keep the percentage
also i am building interpreter through the book. crafting interpreters
how logical would it be to put that on resume?

i was gonna mention that you should revamp your projects, but i had a different reasoning

ideally your projects should be relevant to your role, if you are a full-stack dev, having projects around that is probably a better use of your time and resume real-estate

since you are considering a complex-enough project like an interpreter, maybe a small database / in-memory cache like redis / load balancer would be a better fit. Not to mention the CLASSIC - building an http server from scratch (implementing http on tcp basically)
i am targeting more of backend/systems roles preferebly lower level
hence my projects are toward that side

so i think sending a strong signal that you know your stuff when you are not actually in that role is by building a really intense project in the domain you want to work in and building it in public
